# Lab 2: Day 2

## Exploring Java Data Types and Operators

### Objective
In this lab, you will learn to work with Java data types and operators. You will understand how to declare variables, use primitive and reference data types, and apply basic operators to perform calculations and make decisions.

### Prerequisites
- Basic understanding of Java syntax
- Familiarity with Java Development Kit (JDK)

---

### Step 1: Declaring Variables and Using Primitive Data Types

Declare variables of different primitive data types (byte, short, int, long, float, double, boolean, char) and assign them values. Use the `System.out.println()` method to print the values.
```java
// Declare variables of different primitive data types
byte myByte = 10;
short myShort = 20;
int myInt = 30;
long myLong = 40L;
float myFloat = 50.5f;
double myDouble = 60.6;
boolean myBoolean = true;
char myChar = 'A';

// Print the values
System.out.println("Byte: " + myByte);
System.out.println("Short: " + myShort);
System.out.println("Int: " + myInt);
System.out.println("Long: " + myLong);
System.out.println("Float: " + myFloat);
System.out.println("Double: " + myDouble);
System.out.println("Boolean: " + myBoolean);
System.out.println("Char: " + myChar);
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the values of the declared variables.

---

### Step 2: Using Reference Data Types and Basic Operators

Create an array of integers and assign values to it. Use basic operators (+, -, \*, /, %) to perform calculations on the array elements.
```java
// Create an array of integers
int[] myArray = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};

// Perform calculations using basic operators
int sum = myArray[0] + myArray[1];
int difference = myArray[2] - myArray[3];
int product = myArray[4] * 2;
int quotient = myArray[0] / 2;
int remainder = myArray[1] % 3;

// Print the results
System.out.println("Sum: " + sum);
System.out.println("Difference: " + difference);
System.out.println("Product: " + product);
System.out.println("Quotient: " + quotient);
System.out.println("Remainder: " + remainder);
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the results of the calculations.

---

### Step 3: Understanding Operator Precedence and Data Type Conversion

Use parentheses to override operator precedence and perform calculations involving data type conversion.
```java
// Use parentheses to override operator precedence
int result1 = (10 + 20) * 3;
int result2 = 10 + (20 * 3);

// Perform calculations involving data type conversion
double result3 = 10 / 3.0;
double result4 = (double) 10 / 3;

// Print the results
System.out.println("Result 1: " + result1);
System.out.println("Result 2: " + result2);
System.out.println("Result 3: " + result3);
System.out.println("Result 4: " + result4);
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the results of the calculations.

---

### Summary
In this lab, you have:
- Declared variables of different primitive data types and assigned values
- Used reference data types and basic operators to perform calculations
- Understood operator precedence and data type conversion

### Optional Challenge
Create a Java program that uses all the primitive data types and basic operators to perform calculations and make decisions. Experiment with different operator precedence and data type conversion scenarios.
